title: Reading and Use of English – Part 2
description: For questions 9–16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only ONE word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: For decades, astronomers have been searching for planets orbiting stars (0) other than our own Sun, known as exoplanets. The first confirmed discovery (9) took place in the early 1990s, changing our perspective on the universe. (10) Since then, thousands of these distant worlds have been identified using advanced telescopes. One common method involves monitoring a star for a slight dip in brightness, (11) which occurs when a planet passes in front of it. (12) Although most discovered exoplanets are gas giants, scientists are increasingly focused on finding Earth-like worlds. (13) Not until recently have we possessed the technology to analyze the atmospheres of these far-off planets for signs of life. (14) Provided that a planet is located within the 'habitable zone', it could theoretically support liquid water. (15) In spite of the vast distances involved, space agencies are planning future missions to study these candidates in more detail. (16) Whether or not we are alone in the cosmos remains the ultimate mystery that drives modern space exploration. Each new discovery brings us one step closer to answering this fundamental question. The search continues to inspire generations of scientists to look up at the stars and wonder what else is out there.

example.text: For decades, astronomers have been searching for planets orbiting stars (0) other than our own Sun, known as exoplanets.
example.explanation: Part of the comparative phrase 'other than'.

questions.explanation: Part of the phrasal verb 'take place', meaning to happen.
Adverbial phrase 'Since then' referring to a point in time.
Relative pronoun introducing a non-defining relative clause.
Conjunction used to introduce a contrast.
Part of the 'Not until' structure triggering inversion ('have we possessed').
Part of the linker 'In spite of'.
Conjunction used to introduce a question with two possibilities.
